text: Three thoughts: (1) finding a common set of values is a challenge in itself, (2) in finding our differences in values, we're likely to find the differing underlying contexts and challenges we face, (3) as <@U6KQ2S410> suggests vagueness is a great place to hide differences.

Three thoughts: (1) finding a common set of values is a challenge in itself, (2) in finding our differences in values, we're likely to find the differing underlying contexts and challenges we face, (3) as <@U6KQ2S410> suggests vagueness is a great place to hide differences.

<@UAVRZE1UG> Funny you mention 0.1 + 0.2.  I recall giving a quite the "presentation" to my colleagues when some millions were at stake.  We've been moving our arithmetic to the server ever since.  Leads to the only general principle of interactive systems that I have any confidence in.  Progress is when there's something you used to worry about, but now you don't.  Regress is when there's something you used to never worry about, but now you do.  And reality is the two perpetually come in pairs.

It's great to see various 'manifestos' here. Based on this discussion I'm trying to extract the 'values' from various notes I have - though I'm not sure what qualifies as a 'value' (vs a design goal or principle). One value/aspiration I have is that 'programming should be tangible and live'. The broader theme perhaps is 'dont make me simulate the computer'. My post about that is here: <http://shalabh.com/programmable-systems/wheres-my-simulator.html|shalabh.com/programmable-systems/wheres-my-simulator.html>

I like spreadsheets as a starting point - they have affordances that expose the single level structure clearly and make discovery easier. Also you can represent a few different views of the contents in the same medium, unlike text blob editing. Once you go into a cell you're still back to text though.

I think what I was trying to get at earlier is the contrast between the 'program' and the 'system'. Using 'programs' is only one of many possible way to desing and build a larger 'system'. Could there be other ways to decompose and build/modify a system? Could I, for instance, have a model where I explore a system to easily discover the various componenets (and sub-components and so on), and when I find what i'm looking for, modify it 'in-place'. The 'component' modified here might be very small, e.g. equivalent to a function. This is very different from what it takes to modify a single function in a large running system today. Everything from discovery, explorability to update requires knowledge of various intermediate systems (source control, build systems, compilers, deployment systems, 'the process model').
